titleStmt.title: The Lone Woman and Last Indians Digital Archive

respStmt.orgName: University of South Carolina Provost Humanities Grants
University of South Carolina Department of English
Center for Digital Humanities at the University of South Carolina
Magellan Scholar Program at the University of South Carolina
National Society of the Daughters of the American Revolution
Carnegie-Whitney Grant from the American Library Association

availability.p: Periodicals and books published before 1 January 1923 are in the public domain. If re-publishing, quoting from, or reproducing images from this digital archive, we request that you cite it as follows: Lone Woman and Last Indians Digital Archive, Center for Digital Humanities, University of South Carolina.

Periodicals and books published on or after 1 January 1923 may be protected by U.S. Copyright Law. All attempts to secure permission for republishing post-1923 material in this archive have been made. The names, logos, and identifying images, as well as the content of the articles, remain the property of the periodicals and/or of the authors and artists themselves. Further reproduction, as well as quotation beyond fair use, is prohibited; permission must be obtained from the copyright holder. Archival material published in this archive is used with permission of the institution or library that holds it. Further reproduction, as well as quotation beyond fair use, is prohibited; permission must be obtained from the copyright holder.
Responsibility for any use of the contents of this digital archive rests exclusively with the user.

div.p: "The Daughters of the American Revolution stand for national defense and try to offset the communistic and pacifist propaganda in this country," said Mrs. Alfred Brosseau, of Washington, D. C., president general of the Daughters of the American Revolution at a luncheon held Saturday in the Biltmore, at Santa Barbara by the Santa Barbara, La Cumbre and Golden West chapters of the D. A. R. "Our navy is in a deplorable condition and although we do not want to exceed other nations in our navy program, we must at least equal theirs for our own national defense." Mrs. Brosseau stated that she was cleaning up old snags during her administration, which include the raising of the million dollars for Constitution hall [sic], the memorial to Mrs. Caroline Scott Harrison, first president general; the memorial to the four organizers of the D. A. R., and the student loan fund, which aids those young men and women who have such a desire for education that they will give their first earnings after graduation to repay their debt. The outstanding national need of the D. A. R., she said, is a hall which can accommodate the hundreds of delegates coming to the conventions in Washington. The new $1,000,000 proposed Constitution hall [sic] will meet all the requirements. Mrs. Winfield B. Metcalf announced that a tablet to the "Lost Woman of San Nicolas Island," will be unveiled by the Santa Barbara chapter at 2 o'clock next Saturday afternoon in the garden of the Old Mission. This will be the first public ceremony ever to be held in the garden of the Mission. Permission has been granted by Father Augustine in honor of Mrs. Brosseau. Golden West Chapter of Ventura county is among those invited to attend. More than 110 members were present at the luncheon including Miss Walter B. Meyer and Mrs. James Cerny of Oxnard. At the speaker's table were the following past regents in addition to Mrs. Brosseau and Miss Ethel M. Moss, who presided: Mrs. Arthur Garland, Mrs. Henry J. Finger, Mrs. Guy P. Sawyer, Mrs. Charles Kirk, Mrs. Winfield B. Metcalf, Mrs. Mark Bradley, Mrs. Clara E. Herbert, Mrs. N. S. Kaime and Mrs. Fred Jackson. Miss Elizabeth Mason, regent of La Cumbre chapter, Mrs. O. H. Piper of the Golden West chapter, Mrs. William Paulton and Mrs. C. W. Wells also were at the table. Mrs. Meyer reported a charmingly appointed luncheon, pastel sweetpeas forming the floral decoration.
